task_description:
# Configure Capacitor Android Scheme to HTTPS with Custom Hostname

## Background
A Vite + Capacitor v8 project is pre-scaffolded at `/home/user/myapp`. The mobile app calls a remote backend that whitelists CORS origins on a per-host basis. Because of [Chromium WebView origin truncation on Android](https://github.com/ionic-team/capacitor/issues/6936), the existing custom `app://` scheme is sent to the backend without its hostname (just `app://`), causing every request to be rejected by CORS.

The accepted workaround documented in the Capacitor community is to load the WebView under a standard `https` scheme together with a fixed custom hostname, so that the WebView origin Android emits matches the value whitelisted on the backend (`https://myapp.example.com`).

## Requirements
- Update the Capacitor config so that the Android WebView serves the application from `https://myapp.example.com` instead of the existing `app://localhost` scheme.
- Keep all previously configured Capacitor app metadata intact (the app name, application ID, and web assets directory must NOT change).
- The web project must continue to build cleanly with `npm run build`.
- After the config change, the Capacitor CLI sync step must complete successfully so that the Android native project picks up the new scheme/hostname.

## Implementation Hints
- Read `/home/user/myapp/capacitor.config.ts` to understand the current configuration and the existing app metadata.
- The Capacitor config's `server` section is the place to control the local scheme and hostname that the WebView uses. See the [Capacitor configuration reference](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/config) for the `server.androidScheme` and `server.hostname` keys.
- After updating the config, run `npm run build` and then `npx cap sync` from the project root so that the native Android project is rewritten with the new values.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Project path: `/home/user/myapp`
- `/home/user/myapp/capacitor.config.ts` (or `capacitor.config.json` if you replace it) must declare a `server` configuration that sets `androidScheme` to `https` and `hostname` to `myapp.example.com`.
- The existing `appId`, `appName`, and `webDir` values present in the initial config must remain unchanged.
- Running `npm run build` in `/home/user/myapp` exits with status code 0 and produces `/home/user/myapp/dist/index.html`.
- Running `npx cap sync` in `/home/user/myapp` exits with status code 0.
task_integrations: - None (Capacitor and the Vite frontend run entirely inside the container; no external services are contacted.)
truth:
# Verification Plan

## Setup
- Working directory: `/home/user/myapp`.
- Before running checks, remove any stale build output so the build step is re-exercised: `rm -rf /home/user/myapp/dist`.
- Capture the initial values of `appId`, `appName`, and `webDir` from the pre-bootstrap Capacitor config (already recorded by the initial-state check at `/home/user/.harbor/initial_capacitor_config.json`).

## Verification Steps
1. **Capacitor config exists**: One of `/home/user/myapp/capacitor.config.ts` or `/home/user/myapp/capacitor.config.json` must exist.
2. **Effective config can be loaded**: Evaluate the config with `npx tsx --eval` (when `.ts`) or by reading JSON. The exported default object must include a `server` field that is an object.
3. **Server fields match the requirement**:
   - `server.androidScheme === "https"`.
   - `server.hostname === "myapp.example.com"`.
4. **App metadata preserved**: The top-level `appId`, `appName`, and `webDir` of the new config equal the values captured at bootstrap time from `/home/user/.harbor/initial_capacitor_config.json`.
5. **Build succeeds**: Run `npm run build` inside `/home/user/myapp`. Expected: exit code 0 and `/home/user/myapp/dist/index.html` exists afterwards.
6. **Capacitor sync succeeds**: Run `npx cap sync` inside `/home/user/myapp`. Expected: exit code 0.
